segments.s247-g57: This is a compact, somewhat elliptical clause: همه (all/everyone) is the subject; میگساران (cupbearers) names the group. بیپش is an adjectival/adverbial phrase ‘without precedent’ modifying the group. اندرا is an archaic/poetic item here indicating presence/correspondence (an older copular or locative use) and completes the clause — think of it as a poetic 'were there'. Word order is inverted and condensed as is common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s247-g58: This is a noun phrase describing what they wore: بر (on/upon) introduces the locus 'سران' (their heads); افسر (helmet) is the item located on those heads; از گوهرا is an ablative/genitive prepositional phrase 'of/from jewels' modifying افسر. Poetic order places the preposition before the possessed noun and piles modifiers without an ezāfe, so read as 'helmets of jewels on their heads.'
segments.s247-g59: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing material/origin; دیبای (brocade) is the head noun, followed by the stacked modifiers زربفت (gold-woven/gold-brocade) and چینی (Chinese) — a right-to-left chain of modifiers typical in poetry. قبای (robe) is the outer head that these material/origin phrases modify: 'robes of Chinese, gold-brocade brocade.'
segments.s247-g60: همه (all) is the subject again; پیش گاه (in the presence/place before) forms a prepositional phrase locating the action; سپهبد (spahbed) names the person before whom they stand; بپای is an archaic/poetic verb meaning 'stood/abided' (a form of پاییدن/پاییدن used in the sense 'to stand or remain'). The verb is final, as in classical Persian poetic word order.
segments.s247-g61: همه (all) is subject; طوق (necklace/collar) is the first ornament named and بربسته is a past-participial/adjectival phrase 'bound around' (here effectively 'wearing' or 'with ... bound on'). The conjunction و links گوشوار (earring) as a second ornament. The verb 'to have' is omitted (elliptical), a common poetic compression.
segments.s247-g62: بریشان is a contracted/prepositional phrase equivalent to بر ایشان ('on them'); همه (all) repeats the distributive emphasis; جامه (garment) is the head noun and گوهرنگار (jewel-decorated) is the postpositive adjective modifying جامه. The whole reads as an attributive phrase: 'on them all, jewel-encrusted garments.'
segments.s247-g63: همه (all) quantifies رخ (faces); چو is the simile marker 'like/as'; دیبای رومی is a noun + modifier ('Rûm [Byzantine]-brocade') and برنگ is an adjectival/locative phrase 'in color/hue'. The clause compares their faces' appearance to the colored brocade of Rûm — adjective phrases follow the head in Persian poetry.
segments.s247-g64: This is a pair of participial descriptors: فروزنده (illuminating/radiant/emitting) modifies عود and describes the oud as giving off scent or radiance, while خروشنده (resounding/roaring) modifies چنگ, describing the harp's loud sound. The coordination with و links the two sensory images (smell of oud, sound of harp) that accompany the scene.
segments.s247-g121: بمی is a nisba used substantively meaning someone/from Bam (here 'the Bamites') and functions as the subject. دست بردند is past tense, 3rd person plural of بردن with the light noun دست; دست بردن + باِ + person = 'to take/join hands with (someone)' or 'to join with'. با marks the partner (شهریار = 'the king'). Word order (subject — object noun ــ verb) is normal for Persian, and the verb carries the tense and agreement.
segments.s247-g122: گشاده ('wide/open') modifies the manner expressed by بشادی ('with joy/joyfully'), together giving 'with wide/open joy' or 'rejoicing broadly'. در نوبهار is a temporal prepositional phrase 'in the new spring' (Nowruz), attached as an adverbial of time to the verb phrase. The phrase is poetically compressed (no linking particles), so these words specify the manner and time of the action.
segments.s247-g17: This is a short optative formula: two nominative items joined by و (‘and’) followed by خجسته بواد, where بواد is an archaic/poetic optative/coprimal form meaning “may be” (not simple past). اورمزدت is اورمزد + ـت (the clitic possessive/relational suffix) but here functions as the name ‘Ahura Mazda’ with the pronominal clitic attached in a vocative/benedictory phrase. Word order: Topic (دی و اورمزدت) + predicate (خجسته بواد).
segments.s247-g18: در هر بدی introduces a scope phrase ‘in/with respect to every harm’ (در + هر + noun). بر تو بسته بواد is a passive/impersonal optative clause using بسته (past participle/adjective ‘closed/bound’) with بواد giving the wish ‘may be’. بر here means ‘upon/against’ (i.e. directed toward you); the whole clause expresses ‘may every harm be closed/blocked off against you.’
segments.s247-g19: This segment lists epithets/names that qualify the following idea: دیت آذر افروز و فرخنده روز strings together proper/name-like or adjectival elements (آذر ‘Azar’ here as part of a name/epithet and افروز ‘-afroz’ as an adjectival element ‘illuminating’). فرخنده modifies روز (‘auspicious day’). Word order is a poetic coordination of names/epithets before the noun روز.
segments.s247-g20: تو شادان is a verbless optative-style wish: ‘you [be] joyful’ with شادان functioning as predicate adjective addressed to تو. و connects the second wish: تاج تو گیتی فروز is a nominal phrase where فروز is an adjectival/participial element ‘illuminating’ modifying تاج تو (your crown), and گیتی indicates the scope ‘the world’ (i.e. ‘crown that lights the world’). The coordinate structure gives two parallel blessings.
segments.s247-g21: چو این آفرین کرد is a temporal subordinate clause: چو = ‘when’, این = ‘this’ (referring to the foregoing praise), آفرین = ‘praise/acclamation’, کرد = past tense ‘made/said’. رستم is the subject; بپای is an intransitive verbal/adverbial piece here meaning ‘to wait/stand by’ (a poetic verbal form). Word order: temporal clause + subject + adverbial.
segments.s247-g22: بپرسید is a past-tense verb ‘asked’. و simply coordinates actions. کردش is کرد + third-person object clitic ش ‘him/it’ meaning ‘he made/did it for him/it’; بر خویش جای is a prepositional phrase ‘for himself a place’ where بر = ‘on/upon/for’, خوی + ش together mean ‘his own’ (خوی = root ‘kinship/self’ + ش = his), and جای = ‘place/position’. The sequence lists consecutive actions Rostam performed.
segments.s247-g23: بدو گفت is a two-word light-verb construction meaning ‘he said to him’ (بدو = to him/unto him; گفت here is the past-tense verb ‘said’). خسرو is the addressee’s name or title in vocative position. درست آمدی is a perfective past statement ‘you came rightly/you have come rightly’ (آمدی = you came; درست = rightly/correctly) functioning as the content of what was said.
segments.s247-g24: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the wish or purpose (‘that/may’). از جان تو = ‘from your life’ (از = from; جان = life/soul; تو = your). دور بادا is an optative formula using بادا (poetic optative ‘may it be’) with دور = ‘far’ meaning ‘may (it) be far’. بدی is the object ‘evil/harm’ being wished away: literally ‘may harm be far from your life.’ Word order follows classical Persian optative syntax.
segments.s247-g41: بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning 'he said/commanded.' تا here is the subordinating conjunction 'so that / that' introducing a commanded clause. تاج زرین و تخت is the coordinated noun phrase 'the golden crown and (the) throne' which functions as the direct object of the verb in the following segment; word order places the objects before the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s247-g42: نهادند is the past plural verb 'they placed / they set' (the agents are implied). زیر is the preposition 'under / beneath.' گلفشان is an adjectival or participial description 'flower‑scattering' modifying درخت, so the phrase reads 'beneath a tree that scattered flowers.' Note the poetic inversion: the location phrase (زیر گلفشان درخت) follows the verb rather than preceding it.
segments.s247-g43: همه is the quantifier 'all' modifying دیبه 'brocades / fine silks.' خسروانی is a postpositive adjective meaning 'royal.' بباغ is a locative adverbial 'in the garden' (literally 'to/in the garden') indicating where the brocades were spread.
segments.s247-g44: بگسترد is a poetic past verb 'they spread (out).' The coordinate particle و links to شد 'became,' which is the past of شدن and here means 'became.' گلستان is the subject/complement 'the garden,' and چون چراغ is a simile 'like a lamp.' Together: 'and (the garden) became like a lamp.'
segments.s247-g45: درختی زدند uses زدند idiomatically as 'they erected/placed/ planted a tree' (زدن here = to set up). از بر گاه شاه is a prepositional phrase giving location 'at/from beside the king's court/seat' (بر گاه شاه = the king's place/occasion), with از indicating relation to that place; the subject is still implied 'they.'
segments.s247-g46: کجا is used interrogatively or rhetorically 'where / how' and introduces the clause سایه گسترد 'spread its shade.' گسترد is the past root 'spread' and here takes سایه as its object; بر تاج means 'over the crown.' The final و گاه ('and (at) what time') links this clause to the following temporal clause, leaving the question deliberately open and rhetorical.
segments.s247-g47: تنش is 'its trunk/body' (تن + possessive suffix ش = 'its'). (The text's 'سیم' following تنش is the adjective 'silver' describing the trunk.) شاخش is 'and its branches' (شاخ + possessive suffix ش). ز is the poetic preposition 'of / from' and introduces the materials یاقوت و زر 'ruby and gold' describing the branches. The clause gives material/colour for trunk and branches in compact, poetic order.
segments.s247-g48: برو here is a compact, poetic particle/prefixal element introducing the following adjective phrase (in classical usage it helps mark the quality or coverage); گونه‌گون is the adjective 'multicolored,' خوشه‌های is the plural 'clusters / bunches of,' and گهر is 'gems / jewels.' Together the phrase pictures 'multicolored clusters of jewels' (the verb is implied or understood from context).
segments.s247-g9: همه‌ساله is an adverbial phrase 'every year/annually' placed before the name; اردیبهشت هژیر is a proper name (the month/divinity Ordibehesht together with the name Hojir). There is no finite verb here; the optative sense ('may X ...') is supplied by the verbal formula in the surrounding stanza (implicit or carried by nearby باد in other lines).
segments.s247-g10: نگهبان تو = 'guardian/keeper of you' (noun + second-person object pronoun). با introduces accompanying things ('with'); هش و رای پیر is a coordinated noun phrase 'hush/heed (هش) and counsel (رای) of the elder (پیر)'. Note that هش here functions as a warning/exclamation understood as a nominalized warning; the whole phrase completes the blessing started by the previous segment (the optative 'may ... be' is implicit).
segments.s247-g11: چو 'when/as' introduces a temporal or formulaic clause; شهریورت is the month/divinity Shahrivar (here the subject); باد is the optative particle 'may it be' (classical Persian usage). پیروزگر is a predicate adjective/noun 'victorious, triumphant' describing شهریورت. Word order is subject — optative — predicate, typical in Ferdowsi's blessings.
segments.s247-g12: بنام literally 'by/in the name of' introduces a descriptive epithet attached to the preceding predicate: 'triumphant, by the name of ...'. بزرگی و فر و هنر is a coordinated list of nouns ('greatness, royal glory (فر), and art/skill'), standing in apposition and describing the title or quality invoked. The conjunction و simply links the nouns.
segments.s247-g13: سفندارمذ is the proper name (Spenta Armaiti / the month/divinity). پاسبان تو باد is a short optative clause: پاسبان 'guardian' + تو 'you' + باد 'may it be' (optative). The order is name — predicate — object — optative particle, a compact blessing formula.
segments.s247-g14: This is a nominal wish without an explicit verb: a sequence خرد جان روشن‌روان lists desired goods ('wisdom, life/soul, bright‑soul'); تو is the implied possessor 'for you'; باد again renders the clause optative 'may (these) be (yours)'. روشن‌روان is a compound adjective/noun 'bright‑soul' (poetic).
segments.s247-g15: چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause; خردادت is the proper name Khordadat/Khordad (here with an archaic final -ت as part of the name or older inflection) acting as subject. از یاوران 'from the helpers/allies' marks the source of assistance; بر دهاد is an older poetic third‑person form of دادن ('دَهَد' → 'دهاد') meaning 'gives' or 'places upon'. The whole clause: 'When Khordadat gives (help) from the allies'.
segments.s247-g16: ز مرداد 'from/because of Mordad' uses poetic ز = 'from' introducing the source or occasion; باش is the imperative/optative 'be' used here as 'may you be' or 'be!'; از بر و بوم شاد is a predicate phrase 'glad about/for house and homeland' (از here = 'about/because of' or 'from', بر و بوم = 'house and land' = homeland, شاد = 'happy'). The subject 'تو' is omitted but understood; the line issues a blessing: 'May you be glad in home and country thanks to Mordad.'
segments.s247-g81: چنین غم = “such/this kind of sorrow.” The cluster ب + دین here is a poetic/compound grouping (بـ as a prefixed preposition/particle meaning “by/with” or forming the demonstrative ‘بدین’ = “by/this”), followed by دوده (“dodeh,” literally “smoke/soot”) and نامد (past/passive sense “was named/called”). بنیـز is the enclitic adverb “also/moreover.” Overall this segment states that the sorrow in question was not named/called by this smoke (an idiomatic or poetic expression).
segments.s247-g82: This clause gives a general claim about intensity: غم و درد is a coordinated noun phrase “sorrow and pain.” فرزند is a genitive/possessive modifier: “of a son/child.” برتر ز uses the poetic z for از: برتر = “greater/better,” ز = “than,” and چیز = “thing.” Word order places the comparator after the possessed noun, yielding “the sorrow and pain of a child is greater than any thing.”
segments.s247-g83: بدین کارگر: بدین is the prefixed/poetic combination meaning “with/by this,” followed by کارگر (“worker/active man” or “man of deeds”). تو is the vocative/topic “you.” ببندی is the present/subjunctive/imperative form of بستن (here “you bind/you place”), formed with the classical prefixed بـ. میان “between/in the middle” completes the phrase: “With this doer you will place in the middle.” The verb (ببندی) comes at the end, typical of Persian clause order.
segments.s247-g84: پذیره نیایدت: پذیره (here “reception / refuge/shelter”) is the subject/thing being negated; نیایدت = نیاید + ت (clitic “to you”) means “will not come to you / will not reach you.” شیر ژیان is a vocative/title: شیر = “lion” (a heroic epithet) and ژیان = “living/life” (here “living/lion” or “mighty one”). So the segment addresses the addressee as a ‘living lion’ and states that shelter/reception will not come to him. Note the object/clitic ت attached to نیاید marks the beneficiary/experiencer “to you.”
segments.s247-g85: کنون = “now.” چاره کار = “remedy/solution of the matter” where چاره (remedy) modifies کار (affair/case). بیژن is the vocative name placed in the middle for emphasis, and بجوی is the present/imperative/subjunctive form of جویدن/جستن “seek” (formed with the classical prefixed بـ). The verb-final order makes this an instruction: “Now, Bijan, seek a solution to the matter.”
segments.s247-g86: که = subordinating particle “that/because.” او را uses را as the object marker (“him”); ز توران = “from Turan.” بد آمد is a fixed evaluative phrase “came ill/turned out badly” (بد = unfavorable, آمد = came), and بروی (the verb root رو/رفتن with the classical ب-) here indicates motion/arrival in older usage and links to آمد to describe the coming/arrival. Together: “for he (او) came badly from Turan to you” (i.e., his arrival from Turan was unfortunate). Note the object marker را directly highlights او as affected by the following clause.
segments.s247-g87: ز = poetic/archaic preposition “from/of.” The list گردان و اسبان و شمشیر و گنج enumerates the martial resources: گردان (troops), اسبان (horses), شمشیر (sword), گنج (treasure). The connective و repeatedly joins the items; the whole phrase functions as the source/objects of what follows (things you may take or use).
segments.s247-g88: ببر is the imperative “take!” (classical Persian uses this same form as an order). هرچ = “whatever/anything,” باید = “necessary/you must” (modal), مدار here is poetically used in the sense “do not carry/do not bring” or “do not regard/keep” (the exact nuance is literary/idiomatic), and این برنج = “this rice” (برنج literally “rice,” but in poetic context it can carry the sense of a particular burden/disgrace). So the clause issues the command: “Take whatever you must, but do not take this (rice/disgrace).”
segments.s247-g65: همه دل is a compact subject meaning “all hearts” or “everyone's heart” (همه modifies دل). پر از شادی is the standard predicate ‘filled with joy’ (پر + از + noun). و links this clause to the following participial/nominal description; می‌بدست here functions as a verbless/periphrastic notion of possession/holding (literally ‘in hand’), i.e. ‘having/holding in hand’ (an archaic/poetic phrasing).
segments.s247-g66: رخان ارغوانی و نابوده مست is a coordinated descriptive phrase. رخان describes flowing/loose cloths or veils (here ‘scarves/robes’), modified by ارغوانی ‘purple’. نابوده functions as an intensifier meaning ‘utterly/destroyed/ruined’ used figuratively, and مست means ‘intoxicated’ — together ‘utterly drunk’. The whole phrase describes the state/appearance of the people who held the purple veils.
segments.s247-g67: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning ‘he ordered/said’. تا introduces the following clause as a result/sequence (‘so that’ / ‘and then’), linking to رستم آمد ‘Rostam came’. بتخت is written fused but should be read as به تخت ‘to the throne’ (poetic spelling without space). The clause thus reports the command and its immediate result.
segments.s247-g68: نشست is the past verb ‘sat’ (here third-person singular). از بر گاه is a slightly archaic/poetic locative: برگاه (dais/platform) is the place where he sat, and the prepositional framing is poetic (از ... برگاه). زیر درخت is a plain spatial phrase ‘under the tree’ with زیر before the noun. Together: ‘he sat on the dais beneath the tree’.
segments.s247-g69: برستم here should be read as بر رستم (‘to Rostam’): a prepositional phrase with the preposition بر + the name رستم (written fused). چنین گفت is ‘thus he said’; پس شهریار places the subject (شهریار, ‘the king’) after گفت, a common word-order inversion in epic style: literally ‘to Rostam thus said then the king’ = ‘then the king said thus to Rostam’. Several listed lemma senses do not include this fused preposition+name pattern, so the lexical sense index is not available for some items.
segments.s247-g70: که‌ای is an archaic/vocative particle here (read as که ای / «که‌ای») used to address someone: ‘O …’. نیک پیوند is a compound vocative: نیک ‘good’ + پیوند ‘ally / bond / kinsman’ meaning ‘good/noble ally’ or ‘worthy kinsman’. و به روزگار attaches an adverbial phrase ‘and by fate / in these times’ (به + روزگار) — the line here begins an address to Rostam and the phrase is elliptically continued in the next line.
segments.s247-g71: ز هر بد is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning ‘against / from every evil’ (ز = ‘from/against’). توی پیش ایران stacks two locative/prepositional elements: توی (in/among/for) and پیش (before/in the presence of) + ایران; the idiom denotes ‘for Iran / in front of Iran’. سپر is the predicative noun ‘shield’ (here an exhortation: ‘be a shield’), with the copula omitted in the imperative/wishful sense.
segments.s247-g72: همیشه is an adverb ‘always / forever’. چو is the simile marker ‘like / as’. سیمرغ گسترده پر is a noun phrase ‘the Simurgh with spread-out wings’ where گسترده (‘spread out’) modifies پر (‘feathers/wings’); the sequence gives the image that the addressee should be an ever-present protector ‘like the Simurgh with outstretched wings’.
segments.s247-g25: توی here functions as a locative/prepositional element 'in/among' (classical poetry often uses توی = 'in/among'). پهلوان is the head noun 'hero/champion'. کیان and جهان follow in the classical, ezafe-less sequence to form a genitive phrase: 'پهلوانِ کیانِ جهان' = 'the champion(s) of the Kayanian world.' The ezafe (ِ) is omitted as is typical in Ferdowsi's verse.
segments.s247-g26: This is a compact, antithetical, and somewhat elliptical line. نهان و آشکار are contrasting adjectives 'hidden' / 'apparent'. آشکارت is آشکار with the possessive clitic -ت 'your', giving 'your apparent (aspect)'. The final نهان repeats 'hidden'. A copula (مثل است) is omitted; the phrase reads roughly 'hidden, apparent — your apparentness (is) hidden.' The repetition and omission are poetic devices.
segments.s247-g27: گزین here is used as a nominal/qualifying word 'the chosen/elite' (classical Persian often uses the same form for verb and participial adjective); کیانی qualifies it as 'Kayanian/chosen of the Kayan'. و coordinates the next phrase. پشت سپاه is a noun phrase 'the rear of the army' = 'the army's support' (پشت used metaphorically as 'support').
segments.s247-g28: نگهدار is a noun 'guardian/keeper' used predicatively; ایران is its object (the entity being guarded). و links a parallel role: لشکر پناه is 'the army's refuge' or 'refuge for the host' (again the ezafe is omitted). The sequence lists complementary protective roles.
segments.s247-g29: مرا is the object 'me' (here split in the given tokens as م + را). شاد is a predicative adjective 'glad/happy' and کردی is second-person past 'you made'. بدیدار خویش is an instrumental phrase 'by/with (the) sight/appearance of your own' — بدیدار = به دیدار (by the meeting/sight) and خویش + the clitic ش marks the reflexive/possessive 'your own' (classical forms often attach possessions this way). The verb 'کردی' carries the action 'you made (me) glad.'
segments.s247-g30: بدین is the classical compound 'به + این' meaning 'by/with this'. پر هنر is an adjectival phrase 'full of art/skill' (پر = 'full (of)'). جان بیدار is 'the soul awakened' (جان = soul, بیدار = awake/alert). خویش + ش again supplies the reflexive/possessive ('your own' / 'his own' depending on referent); the whole phrase says that 'with this skill-filled (presence), you aroused/awakened the soul (of the speaker).' The copula is omitted as usual in verse.
segments.s247-g31: This segment is a simple enumeration of names: زواره (here treated as a proper name/place), فرامرز (proper name), و connects, and دستان سام is a name phrase where دستان is presented together with سام (either 'Dastan, (son of) Sam' or 'Dastan of Sam' depending on context). In epic lists like this, proper names are simply juxtaposed with و 'and'.
segments.s247-g32: درستند is third-person plural 'they are correct/intact/well' (classical Persian often uses درستند for 'they are safe/true'). ازیشان (= از ایشان) is the prepositional phrase 'concerning/about them'. چه داری پیام is an interrogative clause: چه 'what' + داری 'do you have' (second-person present) + پیام 'message/tidings' — together 'what news do you have of them?'.
segments.s247-g33: فرو is an adverb/adverbial prefix meaning 'down' (motion or position). بود is the past copula 'was' used here to set the scene (literary past). The sequence رستم ببوسید puts the proper name before the full past-tense verb ببوسید ('kissed'); تخت is the definite object 'the throne'. In sum: 'Down (there) was; Rostam kissed the throne.'
segments.s247-g34: که‌ای is a vocative/exclamatory address (classical contraction of که + ای) used to call or address someone. نامور and نیکبخت are adjectives/appellations placed before the name خسرو; the phrase functions as direct speech or an address to the king: 'O famed, fortunate Khosrow.' Word order is adjective(s) + name in classical Persian poetic style.
segments.s247-g35: ببخت is a vocative adjective meaning 'ill-fated' or 'wretched' used to address someone directly. تو is the addressed pronoun 'you'. هر (with the understood سه) + درستند is a third-person plural predicate 'all three are correct/intact' (درستند = 'they are sound/well'); و شاد adds 'and (are) happy'. The verb درستند is plural to agree with 'هر سه' (the three).
segments.s247-g36: انوشه is a proper name. کسی + کش here forms an agentive compound (کسی‌کش) meaning 'killer/slayer of someone' where کش is the agentive element. کند is the present/subjunctive/optative form of 'to do/make' used to express a wish or report ('may he make/let it be that…' or simply 'may he remember'). شاه یاد is the noun phrase 'the king (شاه) [has] remembrance' with شاه placed after the verb (classical word order allows verb before subject). Literally: 'May the king make remembrance of Anushah, the someone-slayer.'
segments.s247-g37: بسالار is 'to/for the commander' (the prefixed ب- marks direction/indirect object in classical prose/poetry, here addressing the commander). نوبت 'turn' / 'time' precedes the verb; بفرمود is an honorific-polite past verb 'he ordered/said'; شاه, the subject, comes after the verb. Word order is object/time phrase + verb + subject, typical in Persian narrative style.
segments.s247-g38: که introduces the reported command clause ('that...'). The list گودرز و طوس و گوان را is the definite object marked by را (object marker), naming three people; the final verb بخواه is an imperative or hortative 'summon/ask/bring' directed to the addressee. Thus the clause gives the content of the king's order.
segments.s247-g39: در باغ is the locative phrase 'in the garden'. بگشاد (here adjectival/participial) means 'wide/spacious' or 'opened' and modifies the following nominal phrase. سالار بار combines 'year/occasion/occasion for the lord'—بار here has the sense of an occasion/feast/setting connected to the commander (سالار) so the segment describes preparing/spreading a large place for the commander in the garden.
segments.s247-g40: نشستنگهی is a noun meaning 'a place to sit / a seating-place' or 'assembly/seat'. بود is the past copula 'was' describing existence. بس is an intensifier here ('very' / 'exceedingly' in poetic use), and شاهوار is an adjective 'regal, lofty'. Together: 'There was a very regal seating-place.' Note the adjective follows the intensifier, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s247-g73: The paired چه ... چه is a correlative construction meaning 'whether ... or ...'. درگاه ایران is a Persian genitive phrase 'the court of Iran' (درگاه = royal court, ایران = the land/realm). پیش کیان means 'in the presence of the kings' (پیش = before/in the presence of, کیان = kings).
segments.s247-g74: همه is the subject 'all/everyone'. بر در is a prepositional phrase 'at/on the gate/threshold' (بر = on/upon, در = door/gate). رنج بندی is a compound where رنج 'suffering' is combined with بندی (participle/noun from بستن) 'binding' = 'bound in suffering'. میان functions adverbially 'in the midst / amid'. The verb 'are' is omitted (a common poetic ellipsis), so the clause reads 'all are bound at the gate in suffering.'
segments.s247-g75: شناسی here is a second‑person present-form functioning as the verb 'you know' (classical/poetic order places the verb first). تو is the explicit pronoun 'you' (emphatic). کردار گودرزیان is a genitive construct 'the deeds/behavior of the Gudarzis' (کردار = deeds; گودرزیان = the Gudarzis).
segments.s247-g76: به introduces the circumstances 'with/regarding' and sets up a list of contrasting conditions: آسانی (ease), رنج (suffering/hardship), سود (benefit/profit), زیان (loss). The coordinating و links the items; the whole phrase means 'in ease and hardship and in profit and loss.'
segments.s247-g77: میان here refers to the middle/space and with بسته (past participle 'bound/closed') forms میان‌بسته 'closed ranks/ambushes'. دارند is the present plural 'they have / hold' (here indicating they have arranged it), and پیشم 'before me' specifies location. بپای (from پاییدن) means 'to lie in wait/ambush', so the clause describes: 'they have made an ambush before me and lie in wait.'
segments.s247-g78: همیشه is an adverb 'always'. بنیکی ('goodness' or 'with goodness') is the wished-for force; the copula/optative is implied (بنیکی ... رهنمای = 'may goodness be my guide'). م here stands for the copula/auxiliary notion 'be/it is' in archaic/poetic use, را is the object/definite marker attaching to 'مرا' (me), and رهنمای is 'guide' (predicate noun). The full optative sense: 'May goodness always be my guide.'
segments.s247-g79: بتنها (بِتنها) is an emphatic 'only/alone'. تن here means 'person/body' and with the proper name گیو yields 'only the man Giv' or 'Giv alone'. کز is the relative particle 'who/which' introducing a clause ('who was/from the assembly'), and انجمن is 'assembly/council', so کز انجمن = 'who was from the assembly.'
segments.s247-g80: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/against' (here introducing the source or foe: 'from every evil/against every evil'), هر 'every', بد 'evil/harm', and سپر 'shield' is the predicate noun. بود is the past copula 'was' (here rendered as a simple past/copular verb), and در پیش من 'in my presence / before me' specifies for whom he was a shield. The clause means 'he was a shield against every evil for me.'
segments.s247-g49: This is a coordinated noun phrase listing two gems (عقیق و زمرد) followed by the quantifier همه modifying the paired nouns برگ و بار. The sequence noun + همه + noun-noun (برگ و بار) means “all as leaves and fruits/clusters”; برگ and بار are concrete nouns here (leaf, load/cluster) used metaphorically for ornament pieces.
segments.s247-g50: فروهشته is a past/participial adjective meaning ‘scattered/let fall’ used predicatively; it is followed by the source phrase از تاج (“from the crown”). چون introduces a simile ‘like’ and گوشوار is the object of that simile. Word order is participle + prepositional source + comparison: ‘scattered from the crown like earrings.’
segments.s247-g51: همه بار is a noun phrase ‘all the load/ornaments’; زرین is an adjective ‘golden’ modifying that load. ترنج و بهی are two coordinated items naming types of ornament (here read as ornamental roundels and quince‑motifs). The adjective زرین directly precedes the nouns it qualifies (typical adjective–noun order).
segments.s247-g52: میان introduces a locative relation ‘between’; it takes the coordinated nouns ترنج و بهیها as its objects. تهی is a predicate adjective ‘empty/devoid (of)’ placed after the prepositional phrase, so the clause reads literally ‘between the teranj and the beh‑pieces (it) was empty.’
segments.s247-g53: بدو is a directional clitic/particle meaning ‘to it’ or ‘unto it’ and introduces the locative phrase; اندرون is the locative noun ‘inside/within.’ The noun phrase مشک سوده بمی is a compound: مشک ‘musk’ followed by سوده as a proper name (Sūdeh) and بمی ‘of Bam’ as an attributive adjective, so together ‘the musk of Sūdeh of Bam’ sits inside.
segments.s247-g54: همه پیکرش is ‘all his body’ (پیکرش = پیکر + enclitic ـش ‘his/its’). سفته here functions as a predicative past/participial adjective (archaic usage) and is followed by the verb برسان with the postpositive negator نی. In this construction نی negates the verb when attached after it, so برسان نی reads ‘they did not bring/deliver.’ The exact semantic force of سفته is archaic/poetic here; grammatically it is the predicate tied to ‘all his body.’
segments.s247-g55: کرا is an archaic relative/interrogative-like pronoun ‘whom/which’ used here as the object; شاه is the subject ‘the king.’ بر گاه is the locative ‘upon the seat/throne’ and بنشاندی is a past tense verb ‘(he) seated/caused to sit.’ The word order is object (کرا) + subject (شاه) + locative + verb, a common classical arrangement.
segments.s247-g56: برو here functions as a linking adverbial (‘then’/‘thereupon’ / literally ‘go’ in older usage) preceding باد ‘the wind,’ which is the grammatical subject. ازو marks the source ‘from him,’ مشک is the direct object ‘musk,’ and بفشاندی is the past tense verb ‘scattered/sprinkled.’ Together: ‘then the wind scattered musk from him.’
segments.s247-g105: منم is the emphatic first‑person copula/pronoun ('I am' / 'I too') used here to assert the speaker. گوش‌داده is a compound participle/literal 'ear‑given' used adjectivally ('attentive, having given ear') and is followed by the prepositional phrase بفرمان تو ('at/by your command'), so the whole segment means 'I am attentive/obedient to your command.'
segments.s247-g106: نگردم is the negative present/subjunctive/optative form of گشتن ('I will not / I do not turn / become'), expressing firm refusal or resolution. بهرسان here functions as a deverbal/nominal form meaning 'to bring/hand over' (i.e. to betray, literally 'to deliver'), followed by the ablative z + possessor (ز پیمان تو = 'from/away from your covenant'). Grammatically this clause says 'I will not become one who delivers you away from your pledge.'
segments.s247-g107: دل و جان is a fixed compound 'heart and life/soul' (object/theme). نهاده is the past participle/preterite 'having placed / having given' used to mean 'I have devoted.' بسوی is the preposition 'toward,' and کلاه ('helmet/hat') is the goal; together the segment reads 'I have put my heart and life toward the helmet' (i.e. devoted myself to battle/leader).
segments.s247-g108: This clause contains the direction and the reporting verb. بران (literally 'to/thereon') + ره ('the way/road') + روم (here used as a place-name reference 'Rome/Byzantium') gives 'to that Roman road.' کم modifies the speech act as 'briefly / a little' and bفرمود (بفرمود) is the honorific/perfective 'he commanded/said,' with شاه ('the king') as agent in final position — a typical Persian SOV order for reported speech.
segments.s247-g109: و نیز is a simple conjunction 'and likewise.' از پی گیو is a fixed prepositional expression 'in the track/after Giv' (i.e. 'in pursuit of Giv' or 'on account of Giv'). اگر introduces a conditional clause, and بر سرم is a locative phrase 'upon my head' (بر + سر + possessive suffix -م). Grammatically this sets up the condition 'if, in following Giv, [something] fall upon my head...'.
segments.s247-g110: هوا بارد uses هوا in the sense 'desire/passion' and بارد (3rd‑person subjunctive/present of باریدن) to mean 'may fall/descend' — a poetic idiom 'if desire should strike.' آتش بدو is 'fire to him/unto it' (بدو = 'to him/unto it' dative), meaning 'fire may befall me/on account of him,' and ننگرم is the negative present/subjunctive of نگریستن 'I will not look/regard/consider.' Together: 'though desire/fire may fall upon me for him, I will not regard it.'
segments.s247-g111: رسیده is a perfect/passive participle 'have reached/arrived.' بمژگانم is a locative/possession phrase ('at my eyelashes' — بمژگان = 'at/against the eyelashes' with possessive -م), where the poetic image means tears/eyes reaching something. اندر is the preposition 'in/among/against' and سنان is 'spear,' so the image is 'my eyelashes have reached the spear' (a condensed poetic way to indicate danger/tears/close combat).
segments.s247-g112: ز here is the ablative/causal 'because of / from.' فرمان خسرو is the noun phrase 'Khosrow's command.' نتابم is the negative present/subjunctive/optative of تابیدن (here 'to bend / to yield') and عنان ('reins') is the direct object/phrase; together نتابم عنان = 'I will not bend/yield the reins.' The clause thus says 'I will not yield my control/reins because of Khosrow's order.'
segments.s247-g1: چو آمد بر شاه کهترنواز is an adverbial temporal clause: چو + آمد = 'when (he) came'. The postposition بر takes شاه as its object and کهترنواز is an epithet/apposition to شاه (the epithet follows the noun). In older poetic Persian the subject is often omitted and understood from context; word order here is temporal phrase → verb → indirect object.
segments.s247-g2: نوان پیش او رفت و بردش نماز: نوان پیش او رفت is a simple past clause 'a young man/youth went forward before him' (نوان = 'youth/new/young one', پیش = 'in the presence of/before', او = 'him', رفت = past of رفتن). و links the next verb phrase. بردش نماز is idiomatic and compressed: بردش = برد + -ش (took/led him), نماز = 'prayer'; here it means 'led/offered prayer for him' or 'brought him to prayer.' The clitic -ش marks the third-person object. This is older/poetic usage where the verb comes after the object in such compressed expressions.
segments.s247-g3: ستایش‌کنان پیش خسرو دوید is a present/adverbial participial phrase + past verb: ستایش‌کنان is the adverbial participle 'praising' (action concomitant to the main verb), پیش خسرو = 'in front of / before Khosrow', دوید = past 'they ran' or 'they hastened'. The participle sets the manner in which they approached the king.
segments.s247-g4: که introduces the reason/explanation clause here ('for' / 'because'): مهر و ستایش = 'love and praise' (coordinated nouns). مر او را = 'to me / my (toward) him' — مر = 'to me' (dative/ethical), او = 'him', را is the definite/object marker; سزید is a past/past-subjunctive-ish form meaning 'befitted/was due' or 'deserved'. The clause thus says that love and praise were deserved for him (from me). Note the older word order: object-marking را follows the pronoun and precedes the verb سزید.
segments.s247-g5: برآورد سر آفرین کرد و گفت is a sequence of short past actions. برآورد (here) functions as a verb 'he raised' or 'he lifted' (compound verb برآوردن with سر as object: برآورد سر = 'he lifted/raised his head'). آفرین کرد = 'he exclaimed “afarin” / “bravo”' (کرد = did/made), و گفت = 'and said' (introducing direct speech). This is typical narrative past narration with consecutive actions.
segments.s247-g6: مبادت جز از بخت پیروز جفت is an optative/wish clause. مبادت = optative formula 'may it not be' or 'let there not be' (negative wish), جز = 'except', از = 'from/of', بخت پیروز = 'victorious fate/fortune' (بخت = fate, پیروز = victorious used as adjective), جفت = 'pair/mate'. The literal sense: 'May there not be (a) mate except from victorious fortune' i.e. 'May no match be made except one matched by victorious fate.'
segments.s247-g7: چو هرمزد بادت بدین پایگاه is an optative/blessing formula. چو here functions as 'as/like' or introduces the wished-for state; هرمزد = 'Ahura Mazda' (the deity) is the subject of the wish; بادت is a fossilized optative meaning 'may (it) be' or 'may he be granted'; بدین is composed of ب + دین (here representing 'به + این' = 'to this') and پایگاه = 'station/base/post'. Together: 'May Ahura Mazda be at/grant this station.' Note: some constituents reflect older spelling/compounding (بدین = 'to this').
segments.s247-g8: چو بهمن نگهبان فرخ کلاه parallels the previous wish with چو = 'as/like' or continuing the optative sense; بهمن is here the proper name (Bahman, a protector figure), نگهبان = 'guardian/keeper', فرخ = adjective 'fortunate' or epithet, and کلاه = 'hat/crown'. The phrase reads as a wish that Bahman be the fortunate guardian of the crown (کلاه = crown in poetic usage). Word order is noun (name) → title → adjective → object (typical in Persian epithets).
segments.s247-g89: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause; the subject رستم follows it. ز + noun (ز کیخسرو) marks source/aboutness ('on/account of Kay-Khosrow') and is followed by the verb phrase ایدون شنید, where ایدون (archaic/poetic form of 'come/arrive') is the thing heard and شنید is the main past verb meaning 'learned/heard of'. The whole segment literally: 'When Rostam heard of Kay-Khosrow's coming.'
segments.s247-g90: زمین را is the definite object ('the earth/ground' marked by را). ببوسید is simple past 3rd-person 'he kissed'. و links the two coordinated past actions. دم درکشید is a fixed collocation meaning 'drew in a breath' (درکشید here = 'drew in'); درکشید is also past 3rd-person. Word order is object–verb then verb (object already given) joined by و.
segments.s247-g91: آفرین کرد is a light‑verb construction: آفرین 'bravo/praise' with کرد 'did/uttered' gives 'he exclaimed "Afarin!"' or 'he praised'. برو appears before آفرین and here functions as an exclamatory/intensifying particle (archaic/poetic use) rather than the literal verb 'go'; the clause ends with the vocative کای نیک‌نام 'O noble‑named (Kay)'.
segments.s247-g92: چو = 'like/as' introduces a simile. خورشید is the comparison term 'the sun'. هر جای = 'every place' (هر modifying جای). گسترده is a participial/adjectival form 'spread out' describing the scope, and کام (here) means 'fulfillment/satisfaction' (i.e. the thing that is spread). The clause thus praises the addressee: 'Like the sun, your fulfillment/glory is spread to every place.'
segments.s247-g93: ز تو دور بادا is an optative construction: بادا 'may it be' governs the predicate دور 'far, distant' with z + pronoun 'from you' (ز تو = 'from you'). دو چشم نیاز is the noun phrase 'two eyes of supplication/longing' (چشم نیاز = 'pleading/supplicant eyes'). The sentence literally: 'May the two supplicating eyes be far from you.'
segments.s247-g94: This continues the optative wish. دل بدسگالت is the noun 'my/one's ill‑omened heart' (بدسگال = ill‑fated). بگرم is here a jussive/optative form meaning 'may it become warm/ablaze' (in older Persian the b‑ prefix marks volitional/subjunctive). و joins the next predicate گداز, which is a nominal/adjectival noun 'melting/ardor' used predicatively: 'may my ill‑omened heart become hot and melt.'
segments.s247-g95: توی here is an emphatic/addressing particle built on 'تو' (archaic word order), followed by the prepositional phrase بر جهان 'over/on the world'. شاه و سالار و کی is a series of titles ('king and lord and Kay') coordinated by و. The clause asserts the addressee's supreme status over the world.
segments.s247-g96: کیان جهان is the topic 'the Kian (royal) houses/kings of the world'. مر ترا is an archaic ordering where مر = 'to me' (dative/experiencer) and ت + را together represent the 2nd‑person clitic + object marker (effectively 'you' as the reference point). خاک پی is a compound meaning 'dust of the feet' (پی = 'foot/track'), i.e. 'underfoot'. The clause says roughly: 'The world's Kians are, to me, dust under your feet.'
segments.s247-g97: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause ('that'/'for'), and چون here means 'like'/'as' (i.e. 'such as'). تو is the addressee. ندیدست is an archaic perfect/passive‑impersonal meaning 'has not been seen' (literally 'has not been seen such as you'), and یک شاه گاه ('a king/at a time') supplies the referent being compared — together: 'that since a king like you has not been seen'.
segments.s247-g98: نه is the negative 'nor' negating the coordinated terms. تابنده is used substantively ('the shining one', i.e. the sun) and خروشید is the archaic past 'roared/ cried out'; the conjunction و links the second item, گردنده ماه ('the revolving moon'), with the verb ellipsed for the second conjunct (the past verb applies to both).
segments.s247-g99: بدان را is a demonstrative/object phrase ('that one' with the object marker را). ز نیکان marks the source/group 'from the good (men)'. تو کردی is the pronominal subject plus past tense 'you did/made', and جدا is the resultative/adverb 'apart' — together: 'you made that one apart from the good'.
segments.s247-g100: تو داری is present 'you have/possess'. بافسون is a compound meaning 'with sorcery/enchantment' or 'sorcery' used as the thing possessed; و links the next noun phrase بند اژدها where بند is 'fetter/bond' and اژدها 'dragon' — 'you have sorcery and the dragon's bonds.'
segments.s247-g101: بکندم is an archaic first‑person form (بکَندَم) meaning 'I would / I will pull out' or an optative/intentional present. دل is 'heart' and دیو مازندران is the compound 'the demon of Mazandaran' (direct object). The verb takes the object: 'I would pluck out the heart of the Mazandaran demon.'
segments.s247-g102: بفر is an older/poetic verb form meaning 'I will send / I will give' (here part of the same clause of intent). کیانی is the dative/indirect object 'to the Kayanian (the king of the Kayanids)'. و links گرز گران, where گرز is 'mace' and گران qualifies it 'heavy/weighty' — 'and (with) a heavy mace' or 'and the heavy mace.'
segments.s247-g103: مرامادر represents مرا مادر ('my mother' with the object/possessive fused in the older orthography) — literally 'my mother'. از بهر introduces purpose/ reason 'for the sake of', رنج تو is 'your hardship/suffering', and زاد is the past 'gave birth/produced' used with مرا implied: 'My mother bore me for the sake of your hardship' — i.e. 'I was born to endure hardship for you.'
segments.s247-g104: تو باید is the modal 'you must/ought'. که introduces the subordinate clause and باشی is the second‑person singular subjunctive/present of 'to be' (used here as 'be'), so 'you must be ...'. بآرام is an adverbial/phrase 'in peace / calmly' and و شاد adds 'and joyful/happy'.
segments.s247-g113: برآرم is a 1st‑person verb (poetic present/subjunctive) meaning 'I will utter / I will say' and here introduces reported speech. The clause ببخت تو این کار کرد is the content of that speech: ببخت تو is a vocative ('you, wretched one'), این کار is the direct object 'this deed', and کرد is the past verb 'did' (کرد = 'did/made'). The word order places the verb کرد at the end as is normal in Persian narrative; the whole clause functions as the object of برآرم.
segments.s247-g114: سپهبد نخواهم is an elliptical negative predication: the title سپهبد is given as a predicate and نخواهم (1st person sing. present/ imperfect of خواستن) negates it — 'I do not wish to be (or have) a spahbed.' The following نه ... مردان مرد uses an elided copula and the coordinating negation 'نه' to continue: 'nor (do I want to be) مردانِ مرد' (mardân-e-mard, 'the man of men' = the bravest/heroic man).
segments.s247-g115: کلید چنین بند باشد فریب: کلید (subject) + چنین بند ('such a stanza' — بند here = stanza) + باشد (3rd‑person present/optative 'let it be' / 'is') + فریب (predicate noun 'deception'). The verb باشد is used in a general/poetic sense (optative/indicative), so the clause declares that the guiding idea/key of the stanza is 'fereb' (deception).
segments.s247-g116: نه هنگام گرزست و روز نهیب is a negative, elliptical statement where the copula ('is/not') is omitted. نه ... و ... joins two negative predicates: 'not the time of the mace' (هنگام + گرزست — here گرزست is an archaic contracted form = گرز است 'is a mace') and 'not a day for the cry' (روز نهیب — 'a day for the thunderous cry'). In short: this is no moment for force (mace) nor for loud reproach.
segments.s247-g117: چو رستم چنین گفت is a temporal subordinate clause: چو = 'when', رستم is the subject, چنین 'thus', and گفت is the verb ('spoke/said'). This clause sets the time for the actions that follow; the names that follow (گودرز و گیو) are coordinated participants reacting to Rostam's words.
segments.s247-g118: فریبرز و فرهاد و شاپور نیو lists further names followed by نیو ('ney', the reed‑flute). In Persian narrative the verb for 'take up/play' is often supplied by context or the next line; here the sequence names + نیو indicates these men took up or used the ney. The coordinate structure (و ... و ...) simply links the participants.
segments.s247-g119: بزرگان لشکر is a genitive/attributive phrase 'the great ones/chiefs of the army' (بزرگانِ لشکر). برو آفرین are interjectional exclamations — آفرین 'bravo' (and برو here functions as an exclamatory element). The sequence shows the army‑leaders uttering acclamations before the verb in the next segment.
segments.s247-g120: همی خواندند is the past plural verbal phrase 'they kept singing/reciting' (همی marks continuous or habitual aspect in older/poetic Persian). از جهان‌آفرین is the prepositional phrase 'about/of the World‑Creator' (i.e. 'in praise of the Creator'); از here marks the object/topic of their singing. Together: 'they continually chanted in praise of the Creator.'

sentences.s247-s29: All the cupbearers, without precedent, were present.
sentences.s247-s30: Robes of Chinese gold-brocade; all stood in the presence of the spahbed.
sentences.s247-s31: All had necklaces bound and earrings; on them were jewel-encrusted garments.
sentences.s247-s32: All their faces, like Rûm brocade in hue; the oud perfuming and the harp resounding.
sentences.s247-s61: The Bamites clasped hands with the king, rejoicing openly in the new spring.
sentences.s247-s9: May Dēy and Ahura Mazda be blessed; may every harm be shut away from you.
sentences.s247-s10: Deet, Azar-afroz and the auspicious day—may you be joyful, and may your crown illumine the world.
sentences.s247-s11: When Rostam made this praise, he waited (stood by), asked, and made a place for it for himself.
sentences.s247-s12: He said to him, “You have come rightly, O Khosrow; may harm be far from your life.”
sentences.s247-s21: He commanded that the golden crown and the throne be placed beneath a flower-scattering tree.
sentences.s247-s22: They spread all the royal brocades in the garden, and the garden became like a lamp.
sentences.s247-s23: They set up a tree beside the king’s place; where did its shade spread over the crown — and at what time...
sentences.s247-s24: Its trunk was of silver and its branches of ruby and gold; and multicolored clusters of jewels (hung).
sentences.s247-s5: May Ordibehesht Hojir be your guardian every year, with the elder's warning and counsel.
sentences.s247-s6: When Shahrivar comes, may it be triumphant — called by the name of greatness, glory and skill.
sentences.s247-s7: May Spenta Armaiti be your guard; may wisdom, life and bright‑soul be yours.
sentences.s247-s8: When Khordadat grants (it) from the helpers, be glad because of Mordad for house and homeland.
sentences.s247-s41: Such sorrow was not thus called by this smoke; moreover, the grief and pain for a child is greater than any other thing.
sentences.s247-s42: With such a deed you should place the doer in the middle; refuge will not come to you, O living lion.
sentences.s247-s43: Now, Bijan, seek a remedy for the affair; for he came badly from Turan to you.
sentences.s247-s44: From the troops and horses and swords and treasure, take whatever you must; but do not take this rice (this shame).
sentences.s247-s33: All hearts were full of joy; with purple scarves in hand they stood utterly intoxicated.
sentences.s247-s34: He gave the order; Rostam came to the throne and sat upon the dais beneath a tree.
sentences.s247-s35: Then the king spoke thus to Rostam: “O good ally and — by fortune ...”
sentences.s247-s36: Be, against every evil, a shield before Iran forever, like the Simurgh with outstretched wings.
sentences.s247-s13: Among the champions of the Kayanian world — hidden and manifest; your manifest aspect is hidden.
sentences.s247-s14: Be the chosen of the Kayan and the army's rear; be Iran's guardian and the host's refuge.
sentences.s247-s15: You have made me glad by seeing me yourself; with this skill-laden presence you awakened my soul.
sentences.s247-s16: Zowareh, Faramarz, and Dastan of Sam — they are well; what news do you have of them?
sentences.s247-s17: Rostam descended and kissed the throne, saying, “O renowned, fortunate Khosrow.”
sentences.s247-s18: ‘Wretched you!’ — ‘All three of you are well and joyful; may the king remember Anushah, the slayer of someone.’
sentences.s247-s19: The king gave the commander his turn and commanded: 'Summon Goudarz and Tous and Gavan.'
sentences.s247-s20: In the garden they spread open a spacious place for the commander; there was a very regal seating-place.
sentences.s247-s37: Whether at Iran's court or before the kings, all stand bound at the gate in suffering.
sentences.s247-s38: You know the deeds of the Gudarzis, in ease and in hardship, in profit and in loss.
sentences.s247-s39: They have set ambushes before me and lie in wait; may goodness always be my guide.
sentences.s247-s40: Only Giv himself, who came from the assembly, was a shield against every evil before me.
sentences.s247-s25: Agates and emeralds — all as leaves and clusters — were scattered from the crown like earrings.
sentences.s247-s26: All the golden ornaments — roundels and quince‑motifs — and between the roundels and the quinces there was emptiness.
sentences.s247-s27: Within it was the Bam musk of Sūdeh; his whole body was (so) — they did not bring (the) reed.
sentences.s247-s28: When the king set him upon the throne, then the wind blew musk from him.
sentences.s247-s53: I am attentive to your command; I will not turn aside to betray your pledge.
sentences.s247-s54: I have laid my heart and life toward the helmet; the king briefly ordered me to go on that Roman road.
sentences.s247-s55: And likewise, if, following Giv, desire should fall upon my head, though fire may befall me for him, I will not regard it.
sentences.s247-s56: My eyelashes have come up against the spear; I will not bend my reins at Khosrow's command.
sentences.s247-s1: When he came before King Kehtarnavaz, a young man stepped forward before him and offered (or led) him in prayer.
sentences.s247-s2: Praising, they ran before Khosrow, for he deserved my love and praise.
sentences.s247-s3: He raised his head, cried 'Bravo!', and said, 'May there be no mate except one from victorious fortune.'
sentences.s247-s4: May Ahura Mazda grant this station; may Bahman be the fortunate guardian of the crown.
sentences.s247-s45: When Rostam heard that Kay-Khosrow had come here, he kissed the earth and drew in a breath.
sentences.s247-s46: "Bravo!" he exclaimed: "O noble‑named Kay — like the sun, your fulfillment spreads to every place."
sentences.s247-s47: May the two supplicating eyes be far from you; may my ill‑omened heart grow hot and melt.
sentences.s247-s48: You are, over the world, king and commander and Kay; the Kians of the world are, to me, dust beneath your feet.
sentences.s247-s49: That since there has never been a king like you, neither the shining sun nor the revolving moon has cried out.
sentences.s247-s50: You separated him from the good men; you possess sorcery and the dragon's bonds.
sentences.s247-s51: I would pull out the heart of the Mazandaran demon and present it to the Kayanian along with a heavy mace.
sentences.s247-s52: My mother bore me to endure hardship for your sake; you must be calm and joyful.
sentences.s247-s57: I will say, “You wretch, you did this deed.” I want neither the rank of spahbed nor to be called the bravest of men.
sentences.s247-s58: The key to such a stanza is 'deception'; it is neither the time for the mace nor a day for the shout.
sentences.s247-s59: When Rostam said thus, Goudarz and Giv — Fariborz and Farhad and Shapur — (took up) the ney.
sentences.s247-s60: The great men of the army cried 'Bravo!' and continually sang in praise of the Creator.
